formal_statement: In the generic group model over a group of prime order p, any algorithm solving DLP with constant success probability performs Omega(sqrt(p)) group operations.
label: A generic group algorithm operates on encodings of group elements and accesses group arithmetic through an oracle, without exploiting special properties of the representation beyond unique encodings.

formal_statement: In the generic group model over a group of prime order p, any algorithm solving DLP with constant success probability performs Omega(sqrt(p)) group operations.
label: For a group of order n whose largest prime divisor is p, Theorem 1 bounds the success probability of a generic algorithm making at most m oracle queries by O(m^2/p) over random encoding and random discrete-log instance.
Although the full DLP remains out of reach in the example, the improved relation step is reported to threaten some nonstandard oracle-assisted problems on the same curve.
For original ElGamal with a decryption oracle, the paper derives successive secret powers and then applies the p-1 or p+1 algorithms under query and divisor bounds.

The conclusion conditions the speedup on published powers or an oracle returning secret-key powers; therefore the result does not beat Pollard rho for a random single-target secp256k1 ECDLP under the ordinary input model.

The shifting-prime and straight-line description problems are polynomial-time equivalent to ECDLP in the oracle-reduction sense stated by the paper, because ECDLP also makes each partial-lifting task easy.

The proposed reverse-Mestre conditions constrain reductions at several small auxiliary primes in an attempt to bias the fitted rational curve toward larger rank and thereby increase dependence probability.
The authors report no evidence that quadratic residues of the relevant EDS terms exhibit a bias away from one half that would directly expose the hidden multiplier.

An oracle that returns the parity of the minimal discrete logarithm multiplier can be used through repeated point transformations to solve ECDLP with logarithmically many oracle calls and low additional overhead.
An efficient oracle for the defined EDS Residue problem yields an efficient ECDLP algorithm under the source's order and nonresidue conditions.

When lambda satisfies lambda^2+lambda+1 congruent to zero, choosing several small decomposition coefficients first can produce a biased scalar distribution and thereby create a separate cryptographic weakness.
Biased ECDSA nonces can expose long-term keys through HNP lattice attacks.
The authors recovered hundreds of Bitcoin private keys from public signatures.
The study also covers Ethereum, Ripple, SSH, and HTTPS data.

mathlib_area: (not in Mathlib: quantum circuit cost model)
label: The attack handles short nonces and shared prefixes or suffixes.
Repeated nonce reuse gives direct algebraic key recovery.
Knowing a nonce reveals the private key through the ECDSA equation.
Each vulnerable signature yields a modular equation with a bounded error term.
The paper compares leakage attacks with generic Pollard-rho search on reduced nonce spaces.
The advantage comes from nonce bias and does not improve generic ECDLP complexity.
Bitcoin, Ethereum, and Ripple use secp256k1 in the analyzed setting.
Real-world cryptocurrency keys were recovered from public blockchain data.
Multiple independent implementation-failure classes were identified.
Deterministic nonces prevent several bias failures but do not alone prove side-channel resistance.
Signature normalization and protocol details affect preprocessing.
The attack pipeline clusters signatures and tests candidate nonce models.
The work demonstrates real economic exposure from implementation failures on secp256k1 systems.
Hidden Number Problem formalizes recovery of a secret from approximate modular products.
HNP assumes public multipliers and bounded errors involving a hidden value.
Suitable HNP instances reduce to a lattice closest-vector style problem.
Sufficient approximate information about random modular products can determine the hidden number under stated conditions.
The result is not a direct attack on random single-target ECDLP.
Success depends on the multiplier distribution and approximation accuracy.
The lattice embedding converts modular relations and bounded errors into a geometric recovery problem.
The method requires auxiliary information beyond G and Q=[x]G.
HNP later becomes applicable to nonce-leakage attacks because ECDSA yields linear modular relations.
The theorem does not cover arbitrary noisy or adversarial leakage.
Applicability to secp256k1 is implementation-conditional rather than curve-structural.
The paper provides a reusable abstraction linking approximate modular information to lattices.
The attack targets DSA-like signatures when partial bits of many ephemeral keys are known.
The attack operates on signature equations, not on a single ECDLP instance.
Known contiguous nonce bits bound the remaining unknown nonce fragments.
The modular signature equations can be transformed into simultaneous small-unknown congruences.
A lattice is constructed whose close vector encodes nonce fragments and key information.
More signatures can compensate for fewer leaked bits while increasing lattice dimension.
The paper reports successful 160-bit experiments for several leakage fractions.
Very small leakage fractions became infeasible in the reported implementation.
Recovering one nonce reveals the long-term signing key.
The method assumes many signatures under one key and informative leakage per nonce.
No generic sub-Pollard ECDLP algorithm is established without leakage.
LadderLeak exposes probabilistic information about the nonce most-significant bit.
The leakage is less than one full reliable bit per signature.
Fourier-analysis methods are used for weak and noisy HNP leakage.
Standard lattice attacks become ineffective in the extremely weak-leakage regime studied.
Bleichenbacher-style methods tolerate erroneous samples better than standard lattice formulations.
The paper derives time-space-data tradeoffs via generalized birthday/list-sum connections.
Full key recovery is demonstrated for sect163r1 and NIST P-192.
Versions supporting secp256k1 were vulnerable, but the principal full-recovery demonstrations used smaller curves.
The weakness arises from implementation behavior, not abstract ECDLP.
Success depends on group size, leakage probability, errors, data, time, and memory.
Many signatures under the same key are required.
The paper provides optimized HNP implementations and attack records.
The feasible leakage regime is extended beyond earlier lattice-only methods.
Countermeasures require constant-time and leakage-resistant scalar multiplication.
Minerva identifies timing leakage of ECDSA nonce bit-length in a smartcard and several libraries.
Nonce bit-length leakage accumulates across signatures and yields lattice key-recovery instances.
Full recovery is reported on a 256-bit curve using real and simulated leakage.
Signature requirements vary with leakage quality, noise, and parameterization.
The TPM-FAIL comparison reduces required signatures from about 40000 to about 900.
HNP is transformed into CVP-style lattice instances.
Deterministic nonces do not eliminate timing leakage from scalar multiplication.
Each fresh nonce bit-length contributes new information about the same private key.
The paper distinguishes exploitable from non-exploitable leakage.
Lattice success is sensitive to wrong samples and parameter choices.
Real-world 256-bit ECDSA can fail from implementation leakage without solving ECDLP.
Countermeasures include fixed-length processing, blinding, constant-time code, and leakage testing.
Partial ECDSA nonce information can enable recovery of the long-term key under stated assumptions.
The work studies ECDSA modular relations induced by signatures and nonce bits.
The analysis distinguishes leakage of most-significant and least-significant bits.
The recovery is lattice-based and probabilistic.
The guarantee depends on sufficiently many signatures.
Nonce independence and the stated distributional model are assumptions.
No special algebraic structure of secp256k1 is exploited.
The target is key recovery from signatures with side information, not G and Q alone.
The work strengthens the theoretical foundation of HNP-based ECDSA attacks.
Incorrect leakage information can violate the recovery conditions.
Required sample size depends on leakage amount and bit position.
Asymptotic guarantees and concrete lattice behavior must be distinguished.
Applicability to secp256k1 is conditional on suitable implementation leakage.

formal_statement: Let g generate a cyclic group G of order N, and h=g^x with x known to lie in an integer interval of width w. Pollard's lambda (kangaroo) method returns x using expected O(sqrt(w)) group operations, with success probability < 1 (Las Vegas / heuristic).
label: Pollard's lambda or kangaroo method addresses a discrete logarithm known to lie in an interval of width w, with square-root-order work in w and non-certain success.

formal_statement: Let |<g>| = N = prod_i p_i^{e_i}. Combining Pohlig-Hellman with a square-root subgroup search solves DLP in <g> using O(sum_i e_i*(sqrt(p_i))) group operations, hence O(sqrt(p_max)) up to lower-order factors, where p_max = max_i p_i.
label: When the group order factors, a multistage method reduces the dominant work to the square root of the largest prime factor of the order.

formal_statement: Let g generate G of order n and h in <g>. If two walk states coincide, g^{a_i} h^{b_i} = g^{a_j} h^{b_j}, then x = log_g(h) satisfies (a_i - a_j) == x*(b_j - b_i) (mod n). x is uniquely determined when gcd(b_j - b_i, n) = 1; otherwise it is determined modulo n/gcd, leaving gcd candidates.
label: By tracking exponents in x_i=q^(a_i)r^(b_i), a repeated state yields a modular linear relation from which the discrete logarithm can be recovered when the relevant coefficient is invertible, or after resolving a small gcd ambiguity.

formal_statement: For a cyclic group G of order n, Pollard's rho finds log_g(h) in expected Theta(sqrt(n)) group operations using O(1) stored points (no sqrt(n)-size table).
label: Pollard's first index-computation method avoids a square-root-sized stored table and is presented as apparently requiring square-root-order operations.

formal_statement: Pollard's rho attains the same Theta(sqrt(n)) expected time as baby-step giant-step while using O(1) storage instead of Theta(sqrt(n)) storage.
label: Compared with Shanks-style baby-step giant-step, Pollard's rho method retains square-root-order time while eliminating the square-root table.

formal_statement: An elliptic-curve domain parameter tuple over F_p is T=(p,a,b,G,n,h) where p is prime, E: y^2 = x^3 + a*x + b over F_p, G in E(F_p), n = ord(G) is prime, and h = #E(F_p)/n (the cofactor).
label: Elliptic-curve domain parameters over Fp are specified as T=(p,a,b,G,n,h), where p defines Fp, a and b define the curve, G is a base point, n is the prime order of G, and h=#E(Fp)/n.

formal_statement: secp256k1 is the curve E: y^2 = x^3 + 7 over F_p with p = 2^256 - 2^32 - 977 (= 0xFFFFFFFF...FFFFFC2F).
label: secp256k1 is defined over p=0xFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFEFFFFFC2F with curve equation y^2=x^3+7 over Fp.

formal_statement: For secp256k1, the base point G has prime order n = 0xFFFFFFFF...BFD25E8CD0364141 and cofactor h = 1, i.e. #E(F_p) = n.
label: SEC 2 specifies the secp256k1 base point G, its prime order n=0xFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFEBAAEDCE6AF48A03BBFD25E8CD0364141, and cofactor h=1.

formal_statement: In the generic group model over a group whose order has largest prime divisor p, any algorithm solving DLP with success probability bounded below by a positive constant performs Omega(sqrt(p)) group operations.
label: Any generic algorithm with success probability bounded below by a positive constant requires Ω(sqrt(p)) group operations, where p is the largest prime divisor of the group order.

formal_statement: Pollard-rho ECDLP on a group of order n has expected cost O(sqrt(n)) group operations and O(1) space, and parallelizes across m processors with linear speedup via van Oorschot-Wiener distinguished points.
label: The paper summarizes Pollard-rho ECDLP attacks as requiring expected O(sqrt(n)) group operations, negligible space, and supporting efficient parallelization via van Oorschot–Wiener.

formal_statement: Fix a distinguished-point predicate D on G with Pr[D] = theta. Pseudorandom trails are stored only at points satisfying D; two trails that collide reach the same distinguished endpoint, so a repeated distinguished endpoint certifies a collision, with expected extra work O(1/theta) per trail.
label: A distinguished point has an easily tested property; pseudorandom trails terminate at such points, and repeated distinguished endpoints reveal merged trails and hence collisions.

formal_statement: Running m independent rho walks with disjoint collision domains reduces expected time only by a factor Theta(sqrt(m)) relative to one walk, and the total work across processors is not reduced.
label: Running m independent rho walks with independent collision domains yields only about a square-root-of-m speedup and wastes total work.

formal_statement: With a single shared iteration function and a shared distinguished-point database, m processors searching one collision achieve expected wall-clock speedup Theta(m) in the idealized model.
label: Using a common iteration function and shared distinguished-point database allows m processors to contribute to one collision search, giving a speedup by a factor m in the idealized analysis.

formal_statement: For useful-collision probability q=1, the expected work per processor to find a collision in a group of order n is sqrt(pi*n/2)/m steps, plus distinguished-point storage and collision-resolution overhead.
label: For a useful-collision probability q=1, the expected work per processor is analyzed as sqrt(pi*n/2)/m steps, with additional distinguished-point and collision-resolution overhead.

formal_statement: For the summation-polynomial index calculus with factor-base parameter n on the order of sqrt(log q), the expected running time is exp(O((log(q^n))^{2/3})).
label: For n on the order of sqrt(log q), the paper obtains expected time e^{O(log(q^n)^{2/3})}.

formal_statement: The n-th summation polynomial S_n(x_1,...,x_n) of E vanishes at (x_1,...,x_n) in F^n iff there exist points P_i in E(closure) with x-coordinate x_i and a choice of signs such that P_1 + ... + P_n = O (the point at infinity).
label: The nth summation polynomial vanishes at x-coordinates x1,...,xn exactly when signs/y-coordinates can be chosen so that the corresponding elliptic-curve points sum to the point at infinity.

formal_statement: For the random-factor-base prime-field index calculus with practical bound m=4 and factor-base dimension l <= 10, the number of polynomial-system trials is at least about 2^{n-40}; for n > 80 this exceeds the sqrt(#G) generic cost, and each trial costs strictly more than one group operation.
label: With the practical limit m=4 and factor-base dimension l≤10, the number of polynomial-system trials is at least about 2^(n-40), no better than square-root scale for n above 80, and each trial costs more than a group operation.

formal_statement: Let g have prime order in G and let p-1 = prod_j d_j with the d_j pairwise coprime. If for each j the auxiliary powers associated with d_j are available, then alpha can be reconstructed from its residues modulo the d_j via the Chinese Remainder Theorem.
label: When auxiliary powers associated with pairwise coprime factors of p-1 are available, Corollary 1 reconstructs alpha by solving smaller components and applying the Chinese remainder theorem.

formal_statement: The Cheon bounds are minimized at d ~ p^{1/2} for the (p-1) case and d ~ p^{1/3} for the (p+1) case, giving best-case complexity exponents about p^{1/4} and p^{1/3} respectively, when auxiliary input for such a divisor d exists.
label: The paper minimizes its bounds at about d=p^(1/2) for the p-1 case and d=p^(1/3) for the p+1 case, giving best exponents near p^(1/4) and p^(1/3), respectively, when sufficient auxiliary input exists.

formal_statement: Let g have prime order in G, and let d | (p-1). Given g, g^alpha, g^{alpha^d}, the value alpha can be recovered in O(log p * (sqrt((p-1)/d) + sqrt(d))) group operations.
label: If d divides p-1 and g, g^alpha, and g^(alpha^d) are given in a prime-order group, alpha can be recovered in O(log p*(sqrt((p-1)/d)+sqrt(d))) group operations with the stated memory bound.

formal_statement: Let d | (p+1). Given g^{alpha^i} for i over the required range, alpha can be recovered in O(log p * (sqrt((p+1)/d) + d)) group operations.
label: If d divides p+1 and the sequence g^(alpha^i) through the required range is available, Theorem 2 recovers alpha in O(log p*(sqrt((p+1)/d)+d)) group operations with the stated memory.

formal_statement: For curves of fixed genus g, the factor-base (index-calculus) DLP method runs in O(q^{2+epsilon}); with an optimized factor base the asymptotic cost is O(q^{2g/(g+1)+epsilon}).
label: For fixed genus g, the cited factor-base method has O(q^(2+epsilon)) behavior, and reducing the factor base yields an optimized asymptotic O(q^(2g/(g+1)+epsilon)).

formal_statement: For fixed n >= 4 and a curve satisfying the GHS condition, the DLP in the large prime-order subgroup is solved in O(q^{2+epsilon}) time, assuming the kernel of the transfer map is not divisible by that large prime.
label: For fixed n>=4 and curves satisfying the stated condition, Theorem 1 gives O(q^(2+epsilon)) time in the large prime-order subgroup, assuming the transfer map kernel is not divisible by that large prime.

formal_statement: If the GHS transfer sends the input points to divisor classes D_1, D_2 not both zero, then the elliptic-curve relation log_P(Q) is mapped to an equivalent discrete-log relation in the divisor class group (Jacobian) of the descended curve.
label: When the transferred divisor classes D1 and D2 are not both zero, the original elliptic-curve relation can be mapped to an equivalent discrete-log relation in the divisor class group of the descended curve.

formal_statement: In general the MOV reduction is exponential in log(q), because the embedding degree k (the least k with n | q^k - 1) is in general exponentially large in log(q).
label: The paper explicitly states that the reduction is exponential in log(q) in general because the required extension degree k is exponentially large in general.

formal_statement:
Let P have order n and let Q be an n-torsion point such that e_n(P,Q) is a primitive n-th root of unity. Then R |-> e_n(R,Q) is a group isomorphism from

onto mu_n, the group of n-th roots of unity.
label:
With Q chosen so that e_n(P,Q) is primitive, the map R -> e_n(R,Q) is a group isomorphism from

to the n-th roots of unity.

formal_statement: For Q chosen uniformly among n-torsion points, Pr[ e_n(P,Q) has order exactly n ] = phi(n)/n, where phi is Euler's totient.
label: In the randomized construction, the probability that the pairing value has order n is phi(n)/n, so repeated trials are part of the expected-cost analysis.

formal_statement: For a supersingular elliptic curve E/F_q, there is a probabilistic polynomial-time reduction from the discrete-log problem in E(F_q) to the discrete-log problem in the multiplicative group of a bounded-degree extension field.
label: For supersingular elliptic curves, the paper gives a probabilistic polynomial-time reduction from the elliptic-curve logarithm problem to a finite-field logarithm problem.

formal_statement:
For P of order n and a suitable n-torsion point Q in E(F_{q^k}), the Weil pairing e_n maps

into mu_n subset of F_{q^k}^*.
label: For a point P of order n and a suitable n-torsion point Q, the Weil pairing maps the cyclic subgroup generated by P into the subgroup of n-th roots of unity in an extension field.

formal_statement: An elliptic curve E/F_p is anomalous iff #E(F_p) = p (equivalently trace of Frobenius a_p = 1). For this class there is a polynomial-time algorithm for the ECDLP.
label: The paper defines an anomalous elliptic curve over F_p by #E(F_p)=p and announces a polynomial-time discrete-log algorithm for that special class.

formal_statement: For p > 7 and E/F_p anomalous, the discrete logarithm in E(F_p) is computable in O((log p)^3) bit operations.
label: For p>7, the paper concludes that discrete logarithms in E(F_p) can be solved in O((log p)^3) time when E is anomalous.

formal_statement: For an anomalous curve E/F_p and a chosen p-adic lift, the constructed map lambda_E : E(F_p) -> F_p^+ is a group homomorphism, independent of the choice of point-lift, and is either identically zero or a group isomorphism.
label: For an anomalous curve and a chosen p-adic lift, the constructed map lambda_E from E(F_p) to F_p is a homomorphism independent of the point-lifting choice and is either the zero map or an isomorphism.

formal_statement: Evaluating lambda_E at a point requires at most about 2*log_2(p) elliptic-curve additions and has O((log p)^3) bit-complexity under the stated arithmetic model.
label: The explicit lambda_E evaluation requires at most about 2 log_2(p) elliptic-curve additions and has O((log p)^3) bit-complexity under the paper's arithmetic model.

formal_statement: There exists a p-adic lift such that, under the stated coordinate condition, at least one of the two associated homomorphisms lambda_E is nonzero.
label: The paper constructs a nearby lift and proves that, under its coordinate condition, at least one of the two associated homomorphisms is nonzero.

formal_statement: The constructed map lambda_E is identically zero iff (equivalently) the lifted p-adic curve has a nonzero p-torsion point, among the stated equivalent conditions.
label: The paper proves equivalent conditions for the constructed map to be zero, including the existence of a nonzero p-torsion point in the lifted p-adic curve.

formal_statement: Under the stated assumptions with known group order, computing [p]P and [p]Q each takes O(log p) group operations, yielding the linear-time (in log p) cost in basic group operations.
label: Under the stated assumptions and known group order, the nontrivial scalar multiplications [p]P and [p]Q require O(log p) group operations, giving the paper's linear-time characterization in basic group operations.

formal_statement: For an anomalous curve, log_P(Q) mod p equals the quotient psi([p]Q)/psi([p]P) where psi is the p-adic elliptic logarithm of the formal group, provided psi([p]P) is a unit.
label: The p-adic logarithm relation yields the discrete logarithm modulo p as a quotient of the logarithms of [p]P and [p]Q.

formal_statement: Lift P,Q in E(F_p) to points in E(Q_p); multiply by p to land in the formal-group neighborhood (kernel of reduction); apply the p-adic elliptic logarithm to obtain a Z_p-linear relation that determines log_P(Q) mod p.
label: The method lifts the input points to E(Q_p), multiplies by p to enter the formal-group neighborhood, and applies the p-adic elliptic logarithm to obtain a linear relation for the discrete logarithm.

formal_statement: #E(F_p) = p iff the trace of Frobenius a_p = 1 (since #E(F_p) = p + 1 - a_p).
label: The paper studies prime-field elliptic curves with #E(F_p)=p, equivalently trace of Frobenius equal to one.

formal_statement: The shifting-prime problem: given the reduction modulo p of a point P, and a second prime r, output the reduction modulo r of some rational lift of P.
label: The shifting prime problem asks for the reduction modulo a second prime r of any rational lift of a given point from the reduction modulo p.

formal_statement: The number of r-tuples from a cyclic subgroup of F_q^* (of size N) that admit rational lifts of rank <= r and bounded height h is O(C(r,h) * N^r), an exponential-in-(r,h) factor times N^r, rather than N^{r+1}.
label: The number of tuples from a cyclic finite-field subgroup that admit rational lifts of bounded rank and height is bounded by a factor exponential in powers of r and h times N to the r, rather than N to the r+1.

formal_statement: If lifted rational points satisfy a nontrivial integer linear relation sum c_i P_i = O whose coefficient combination is invertible modulo the subgroup order n, then this relation determines log_P(Q) modulo n.
label: If the lifted rational points satisfy a nontrivial integer linear relation whose corresponding coefficient combination is invertible modulo the subgroup order, the relation yields the discrete logarithm of Q to the base P.

formal_statement: The EDS discrete-log problem admits baby-step giant-step and Pollard-rho generic searches, so its cost retains the Theta(sqrt(n)) baseline unless additional structure is exploited.
label: Baby-step giant-step and Pollard-rho style generic searches apply to EDS Discrete Log, so the recurrence formulation retains square-root baselines unless additional exploitable structure is found.

formal_statement: For the associated net polynomials, the term indexed by (v_1,...,v_k) vanishes exactly when sum_i v_i P_i = O, outside the normalized exceptional index set.
label: Associated net polynomials vanish exactly when the corresponding integer linear combination of the input elliptic-curve points is the identity, outside the stated normalized exceptional indices.

formal_statement: The period of the unnormalized elliptic divisibility sequence equals ord(P) * (multiplicative order in the base field of the associated scaling factor).
label: The period of the unnormalized EDS is the point order multiplied by the multiplicative order of a scaling factor in the base field.

formal_statement: Restricting an elliptic net W: A -> R to a subgroup B <= A, or precomposing W with a homomorphism of free abelian groups, again yields an elliptic net.
label: Restricting an elliptic net to a subgroup of its index lattice, or precomposing with a homomorphism of free abelian groups, produces another elliptic net.

formal_statement: For an associated nondegenerate elliptic net, the term indexed by v vanishes iff the indexed integer combination sum_i v_i P_i equals the identity O.
label: For an associated nondegenerate net, a term vanishes exactly when the indexed integer combination of the defining points is the identity.

formal_statement: An endomorphism of E is a rational map phi: E -> E with phi(O)=O. If phi is defined over the base field K, then phi is a group homomorphism on E(L) for every extension L/K.
label: An endomorphism of an elliptic curve is a rational self-map preserving the identity; when it is defined over the base field it acts as a group homomorphism on rational points over that field and its extensions.

formal_statement: Applying the extended Euclidean algorithm to (n, lambda) yields a vector (a,b) in the kernel lattice { (a,b) : a + b*lambda == 0 (mod n) } with |a|,|b| = O(sqrt(n)) and Euclidean norm O(sqrt(n)).
label: The extended Euclidean algorithm applied to n and lambda constructs a kernel vector with components below square-root scale and Euclidean norm bounded on the order of sqrt(n).

formal_statement: The q-power Frobenius map pi_q: (x,y) |-> (x^q, y^q) is an endomorphism of E/F_q; over extension fields with a normal basis it is computable as a cyclic shift.
label: The Frobenius q-power map is an endomorphism and can be especially cheap over extension fields, for example becoming a cyclic shift when a normal basis is used.

formal_statement: If the characteristic polynomial of an endomorphism phi has a root lambda modulo the prime subgroup order n, then phi acts on that subgroup as the scalar multiplication map [lambda].
label: The method assumes that the characteristic polynomial of the endomorphism has a root lambda modulo the prime subgroup order n, so that the endomorphism acts as the scalar map [lambda] on that subgroup.

formal_statement: On a prime-order-n subgroup admitting an order-3 endomorphism, that endomorphism acts as [lambda] where lambda^2 + lambda + 1 == 0 (mod n).
label: On a prime-order subgroup, the order-three endomorphism acts as multiplication by an eigenvalue lambda satisfying lambda^2+lambda+1 congruent to zero modulo the subgroup order.
For arbitrarily large prime group orders, the paper proves that the proposed walk class can attain the random-case performance using probability results for walks in Z/pZ.
The total expected cost is modeled as p·m^m/s^m times T(E,m,V), where T is the cost of solving the final polynomial system.

The proposed prime-field variant constructs the factor base incrementally from random points R=uP+vQ, making each collected point a trivial relation instead of repeatedly solving point-decomposition systems.
Assuming its single-system cost T is comparable to the per-system cost T' in the Petit et al. method, the proposal asymptotically improves that method for fixed m as p grows.
A single summation-polynomial system is solved to find a dependency among the accumulated factor-base points, replacing both repeated Gröbner computations and the usual sparse linear-algebra stage.
The conclusion states that the complexity of the single polynomial system is poorly understood and requires deeper analysis, including whether non-Gröbner methods are better suited.
The decomposition algorithm lists all isolated decompositions in expected time polynomial in e^{n^2}·log(q).
The factor base is defined by a degree-two covering φ:E→P1 and consists of points whose image lies in P1(Fq).
Diem proves an expected running-time bound e^{O(max(log q,n^2))} for ECDLP over F(q^n).
The relation-generation loop therefore needs O(q^{1/2}) decomposition attempts on average under the stated parameter conditions, and the complete algorithm is polynomially bounded in q.
If n→∞ and n/log(q)→0, the ECDLP over F(q^n) is solvable in expected time (q^n)^{o(1)}.
Under (2+ε)n^2≤log2(q) and sufficiently large n, a uniformly random point has an isolated decomposition with probability at least q^{-1/2}.
The new decomposition problem can be solved in expected time polynomial in e^{mn}·log(q), and the number of isolated decompositions of a point is at most e^{O(mn)}.
Diem's strengthened theorem gives expected time e^{O(max(log q, n·sqrt(log q), n^{3/2}))} for ECDLP over F(q^n).

The new algorithm avoids summation polynomials and projection to products of projective lines; it uses direct-sum subspaces of F(q^n) and decompositions P=P1+...+Pm with Pi drawn from distinct factor-base components.
The paper explicitly states that it cannot establish the desired expected running time for all ECDLP instances.
Under the paper's size conditions, a uniformly random point has an isolated decomposition with probability 1/e^{O(mn)}.
For extension degrees between constants times (log q)^{1/3} and log q, the paper gives expected time e^{O(log(q^n)^{3/4})}.

Gaudry gives a general index-calculus framework for fixed-dimensional abelian varieties: choose a one-dimensional factor base, generate decompositions, collect relations, and solve sparse linear algebra.
For the dimension-one factor base and n-term decompositions, the expected number of decompositions per random target approaches 1/n! as q grows.

For a fixed family and fixed dimension, one relation can be found by a Gröbner-basis computation followed by univariate factorization in time polynomial in log(q), with exponential dependence hidden in fixed structural parameters.
The hidden constant grows rapidly with the extension degree, so the attack is presented as relevant only to small-degree extension fields.
A related characteristic-two proposal replaces bounded integers by low-degree polynomials over F2 satisfying a summation-polynomial congruence.
The proposed speedup is conditional on an algorithm that finds bounded solutions of explicit modular multivariate polynomial equations in polynomial or low-exponential time.
The paper explicitly leaves open whether the required bounded solutions for the proposed equations can be found in polynomial or low-exponential time.
For E/Fp, Semaev proposes generating a random point R=l1P+l2Q and seeking bounded solutions of fn+1(x1,...,xn,x(R))=0 mod p to obtain relations among small-coordinate points and the input points.
Summation polynomials can be constructed recursively by resultants; fn is symmetric, has degree 2^(n-2) in every variable for n≥3, and is absolutely irreducible.
The first-fall-degree assumption is described as potentially too optimistic; last-fall degree is more algorithm-independent and precise, but substantially harder to bound for ECDLP systems.
Even after combining large symmetry groups and invariant coordinates in the cited binary-field work, the resulting algorithms were reported as not competitive with Pollard rho.
The surveyed summation-polynomial approaches were not practical at contemporary cryptographic key sizes even under optimistic assumptions, with both time and memory extremely high.
The survey distinguishes proven algorithms from complexity claims and focuses on unresolved questions surrounding summation-polynomial index calculus.

Symmetric variables can reduce the number of solutions and sometimes polynomial degree, but after Weil descent the invariant variables may occupy much larger vector spaces when the factor-base subspace is not multiplicatively closed.
The invariant-theory argument does not transfer directly to characteristic two when the field characteristic divides the acting group order; obtaining analogous results there is left open.

Direct construction of high summation polynomials remains a bottleneck; the authors report practical computation only up to the sixth and ask for alternative PDP systems for extension degree at least six.
For twisted Edwards and twisted Jacobi-intersection representations over nonbinary extension fields, curve symmetries are used to reduce the point-decomposition problem by an exponential factor.
If phi is equivariant under translation by an m-torsion point, the corresponding summation polynomial has additional invariance beyond permutation symmetry.

A factor base invariant under an order-m torsion translation can be quotiented by that action, reducing its size by m and the sparse linear-algebra dimension accordingly, for an m^2 linear-algebra speedup.
When inversion and full rational 2-torsion are compatible with the chosen morphism, the factor base can in favorable cases be reduced by a total factor of eight.
Summation polynomials are generalized from the x-coordinate map to arbitrary morphisms phi:E→P1, allowing factor bases and relation equations adapted to torsion symmetries.
The framework is stated to extend to higher-order torsion points, but the paper's practical focus and detailed results are primarily for 2-torsion.

Using invariant variables and sparse interpolation, the authors compute the eighth symmetrized summation polynomial in about 40.5 CPU-hours, whereas direct approaches had been stopped after at least a month.
The descended systems have an affine multihomogeneous block structure, and the proposed Sub-Macaulay method generates many controlled low-degree equations before linearization.

For binary curves, the point-decomposition step is reduced to solving a multivariate equation over F(2^n) with all variables restricted to a chosen F2-vector subspace, followed by Weil descent to Boolean equations.

The complexity analysis depends on Assumption 1: with probability exponentially close to one, the generated linear equations have the required rank and their linearized solutions correspond to actual solutions.
For binary Edwards curves, the coordinate t=x+y is invariant under a relatively large automorphism group and yields lower-degree summation-polynomial representations.
The paper concludes that, for practical prime-degree binary fields, summation-polynomial methods remain slower than Pollard rho, even for static-Diffie-Hellman variants requiring only one relation.
SAT solvers handled larger factor-base dimensions and often used less memory than Gröbner methods, but runtime varied strongly and no-solution instances were usually slow.
For larger tested n, symmetry-breaking systems often had degree of regularity 3 rather than 4, though the reason was unclear.
Greater invariant symmetry significantly reduced runtime and slightly reduced degree of regularity in the reported m=3 and m=4 Gröbner experiments.
Experiments keep first fall degree fixed at 2 while degree of regularity rises from 3 to at least 5 as n increases to 40, with memory exceeding 38 GB.
For the third summation-polynomial system after Weil descent, the first fall degree is usually 2, not the earlier upper bound 5.
Under Assumption 3.3, deciding whether a high-order elliptic-curve summation polynomial evaluates to zero is NP-complete.
Repeating the Semaev-2015-style experiment at n=45,m=2,t=2 gave degree of regularity 5 and used about 126 GB, contradicting the constant-four extrapolation at that parameter.
For a fixed odd prime, the analogous evaluation problem for summation polynomials attached to a specified singular curve is unconditionally NP-complete.
For ordinary binary elliptic curves, a trace expression in the x-coordinate defines a surjective group morphism to F2 and yields a linear relation inside the descended ideal.
The trace morphism can be added explicitly to the relation-generation equations or used to restrict search to its kernel, potentially speeding Gröbner computations.
With the APS factor-base size s=p^(1/m), the expected relation probability requires several independently generated factor bases and therefore several Gröbner-basis computations, not necessarily one.
Algorithm 3.1 removes Gröbner-basis solving by enumerating candidate factor-base tuples and evaluating the summation polynomial directly.
Small-prime experiments rank Algorithm 4.1 fastest overall, show direct evaluation can outperform Gröbner methods, and report lower memory plus easy parallelism for the non-Gröbner variants.
A recursive determinant/resultant evaluation method evaluates Sm in O(log^2 p) bit operations for small fixed m, allowing evaluation of S9 and S10 without explicitly constructing those polynomials.
The prime-field algorithms use a random, target-dependent factor base consisting of points aiP+biQ; rerunning the algorithm changes the factor base.
Algorithm 4.1 avoids summation polynomials, Gröbner bases, and linear algebra by summing m-1 factor-base points and testing whether the resulting point lies in the factor base.
Assumption 1 states that, except for a negligible fraction of systems, degree of regularity equals first fall degree plus a lower-order term.
For the considered Weil-descent system, the first fall degree is at most m*t+1.
Assumption 1 states that the maximum F4 step degree is at most 4 for the split Boolean systems with the stated factor-base dimension.
After Weil descent in characteristic two, the split formulation produces cubic Boolean systems rather than the much higher-degree direct summation equation.
In 2300 tested systems over small parameter ranges, F4 reached degree at most 4 before the basis was obtained, supporting Assumption 1 within the tested range.
The paper reports that degree of regularity generally exceeds 4 when the factor-base dimension k is increased beyond ceil(n/m), even though first fall degree remains 4.
The proposed algorithm replaces one high-degree summation-polynomial equation with a chain of third summation-polynomial equations using intermediate variables.

Reducing n' by one costs a factor 2^m in decomposition probability and a factor 2 in the number of required relations, so the Gröbner computation must improve by more than 2^(m-1) for the Delta step to be beneficial.
The Delta method deliberately chooses factor-base dimension n' below n/m, sacrificing relation probability but often reducing Gröbner-basis time and memory dramatically.
For the tested range, standard hybrid variable fixing outperformed block-hybrid fixing, and combining hybrid with Delta gave no significant improvement over Delta alone.
Magma's PairsLimit parameter can reduce memory and runtime for difficult F4 computations, but no formula is known for selecting its optimum value.
The study reproduces and extends Petit-Quisquater degree-of-regularity experiments from n=11,17 up to n=29 and generally observes degrees below the assumed m^2+1 bound.
For even n and m=2, choosing a subfield-based factor-base basis produced extremely large practical speedups and degree of regularity 3 for every tested solvable system.
At n=40 the reported Gröbner time fell from roughly 375-388 seconds with the standard basis to 0.02-0.08 seconds with a subfield basis.
The paper concludes that the true complexity of the Semaev/Weil-descent/Gröbner index-calculus method remains undetermined.
Matrix compression choices trade CPU cost against memory use; the paper discusses no compression, bitmap compression, and index-based representations for sparse rows.
The paper proves termination and that the stated F4 algorithm returns a Groebner basis generating the same ideal as the input.
F4 is designed to be efficient for degree-reverse-lexicographic ordering, while efficient conversion to a lexicographic basis is delegated to another algorithm.
Computing a Groebner basis is only one stage of a full polynomial-solving process; change of ordering, triangular decomposition, and root extraction can remain necessary afterward.
The selection strategy is described as crucial for performance, and selecting critical pairs of minimal degree is presented as a practical strategy for controlling matrix construction.

The F4 paper explicitly states that the algorithm does not improve the worst-case complexity of Groebner-basis computation, despite being several times faster than earlier implementations on reported examples.
The paper identifies open optimization directions in linear algebra, matrix compression, selection strategies, multihomogeneous weights, and exploitation of symmetries.

A reported integer-coefficient computation required about 18 days and produced roughly 1660 MB of output containing 1344 large polynomials; the paper notes that the output was too large to use and interprets this as approaching intrinsic Groebner-basis complexity.
The paper warns that the efficiency of F4 cannot be fully appreciated from small examples.
Critical-pair and reductor selection strategies materially affect performance, and common sugar-style strategies impose sequential dependencies that hinder massive parallelization.
The paper proves correctness up to degree d and gives a termination argument under homogeneous input and no reduction to zero, while noting that a modification can ensure termination more generally.
The main F5 algorithm is incremental: it processes a list of homogeneous polynomials and constructs Groebner bases for nested ideals one input polynomial at a time.
The F5 correctness criterion is expressed using admissible signed polynomials, normalized critical pairs, and signature-compatible reductions.

The paper states that F5 is not always faster than F4 and expects poor performance on overconstrained systems; the largest benefits are expected when the number of equations does not exceed the number of variables.
The IsReducible step requires ordinary leading-term divisibility, satisfaction of the new normalization criterion, absence of an applicable rewrite rule, and avoidance of an identical matrix row.
The paper proves that a regular-sequence input produces no reduction to zero, while repeated or structurally dependent equations can still create reductions to zero in non-regular cases.
F5 augments polynomials with signatures and proposes a criterion that eliminates useless critical pairs; for a regular-sequence input, the paper states that no useless critical pairs are generated.
Signatures assign a unique provenance to generated polynomials and allow the algorithm to recognize and reuse computations already performed at lower degrees.
When F5 encounters a reduction to zero, the paper links its signature to a syzygy not generated by the principal syzygies used by the criterion.
Gaudry assumes the descended algebraic set is generically zero-dimensional, after which rational solutions can be obtained by a Groebner basis followed by roots of univariate polynomials.

For prime-field Semaev decomposition, all index-calculus ingredients are available except efficient factor-base decomposition; the book notes that no efficient method was known for the bounded summation-polynomial equation even at n = 5.

For integer polynomials in three or more variables, known small-root methods may produce several polynomials sharing the root, but there is no general guarantee that these polynomials are algebraically independent.
The attack stage uses hyperelliptic index calculus with relation collection over a factor base followed by sparse linear algebra.
For a short Weierstrass anomalous curve, Corollary 3.6 gives an explicit procedure over Z/p^2Z to evaluate lambda_E using a lifted point and the multiple (p-1)A.
If a finite-field point is liftable in the specified setting, at least one rational lift has projective coordinates with a straight-line program of polylogarithmic length in the input parameters.
The height decision problem asks whether a finite-field point has any rational lift on the chosen rational curve whose naive height is at most a supplied bound.

mathlib_area: (not in Mathlib: quantum circuit cost model)
label: Although a minimal-height rational lift may have coordinates requiring exponentially many bits when expanded, scalar multiplication can give those coordinates a short straight-line description.
The paper replaces explicit rational point lifting with three partial-information problems and reduces ECDLP to each of them under stated rank-distribution assumptions.
The properly reduced projective coordinates of a scalar multiple mP admit a straight-line program whose length is polynomial in the logarithms of the curve coefficients and m.
The straight-line description problem asks for polynomial-length straight-line programs representing projective coordinates of any rational lift of the finite-field point.
The paper explicitly states that its asymptotic analysis leaves open the possibility that lifting or xedni techniques could behave differently for lower finite-field sizes.

The tuple-count obstruction is formulated independently of the particular procedure used to construct the rational curve and its lifts, provided the resulting objects satisfy the stated rank and height bounds.

A lifting-based index-calculus approach to ECDLP faces two distinct obstacles: constructing a rational lift of sufficiently large rank and lifting finite-field points to rational points of reasonably bounded height.
The paper derives necessary conditions and obstructions for lifting-based attacks; it does not provide an algorithm that solves random single-target ECDLP on a prime-field curve.

For a rational elliptic curve of rank at most r, any r+1 rational points of canonical height at most h satisfy a nontrivial integer relation with coefficients bounded in terms of r and h divided by the logarithm of the minimal discriminant.
A xedni-style method that lifts only a fixed bounded number of random points has asymptotically negligible success probability under the paper's assumptions.
For a dependent lifted tuple satisfying the stated height and discriminant conditions, there is a nonzero integer relation whose coefficients are bounded by an absolute constant independent of p.
The authors implemented the xedni experiments using LiDIA and SIMATH and measured dependence behavior on generated rational lifts.

The xedni construction lifts several finite-field points to rational points and then constructs an elliptic curve over the rationals passing through those lifts while reducing to the original curve modulo p.
Combining the bounded relation coefficients with the probability count gives an asymptotic requirement of order p trials for the analyzed xedni attack.
The numerical experiments and asymptotic analysis jointly support the conclusion that the analyzed xedni calculus is impractical for cryptographic-size prime fields.
The attack begins with a bounded number of random linear combinations of the input points P and Q, most plausibly four to six in the parameter discussion, and attempts to lift those combinations.
Reverse-Mestre local conditions occurred more frequently among dependent samples in some experiments, but the measured effect was far too small to overcome the low absolute dependence probability.

The paper's negative conclusion is directed at the xedni construction based on a bounded collection of rational lifts and does not constitute a theorem that every possible lifting-based ECDLP method must fail.
Under the height and discriminant assumptions stated in the analysis, the probability that one xedni trial produces a useful dependence is bounded above by a constant divided by p.
The paper defines EDS Association, EDS Residue, and width-s EDS Discrete Log as sequence problems tied to the unknown multiplier in an elliptic-curve discrete logarithm instance.
An elliptic net is a map from an integer lattice to a field satisfying a four-term nonlinear recurrence, and rank-one elliptic nets are elliptic divisibility sequences.
An EDS term at index t can be computed using logarithmically many recurrence steps, with bit complexity polynomial in log t and log q in the stated field-operation model.

EDS Association is trivial for a perfectly periodic normalization but hard in the non-perfect case, whereas EDS Discrete Log can reduce to a finite-field DLP in the non-perfect case and remains the hard variant in the perfect case.
When the point order is greater than three and coprime to q−1, an explicitly rescaled associated EDS is perfectly periodic with period equal to the point order.
For the perfectly periodic normalization, the sequence value at index n can be computed from the curve point [n]P without first knowing n.

The EDS-residue route requires explicit arithmetic conditions including odd order, coprimality with q−1, and a nonresidue scaling factor; these conditions are logically separate from the existence of the EDS itself.
Under the proposition's conditions, quadratic residuosity of a specified product of EDS terms reveals the parity of the hidden multiplier when the scaling factor is a quadratic nonresidue.
Three consecutive suitable EDS terms determine the x-coordinate of the corresponding multiple [n]P.
The net-polynomial construction extends from complex uniformization to arbitrary base fields through algebraic formulas with controlled coefficient rings.

The paper establishes an explicit isomorphism between scale-equivalence classes of nondegenerate elliptic nets and equivalence classes of cubic curves with appropriate marked points, preserving inclusion and relation structure.
Degenerate elliptic nets and singular boundary cases are not fully covered by the main classification, and extending the correspondence to them is left as further work.
An elliptic net is a map from a finitely generated free abelian group to an integral domain, vanishing at zero and satisfying the stated quartic recurrence for every four lattice inputs.
Subject to nonvanishing conditions, a finite collection of initial values determines every term of a rank-one, rank-two, or higher-rank elliptic net through the recurrence and Laurent formulas.
A normalized nondegenerate elliptic net determines an associated cubic curve and marked points, uniquely up to the stated unihomothetic change of variables.
The recurrence expressions for net terms have a Laurent property in finitely many initial terms, yielding integrality statements when those seeds lie in an appropriate integral domain.
Net polynomials are rational functions on a product of elliptic curves whose zeros encode prescribed integer linear relations among the input points.
For nondegenerate rank-two nets, explicit rational formulas reconstruct a Weierstrass cubic and two marked points from a finite set of net values.
Rank-one elliptic nets recover elliptic divisibility sequences, while higher-rank nets encode several points and their integer linear combinations.
Elliptic nets related by the specified quadratic scaling represent the same underlying curve-point data up to the paper's equivalence, and normalized representatives remove this freedom.
The normalized collection of net polynomials satisfying the recurrence and divisor conditions is uniquely determined.

The proposed acceleration is advantageous only when evaluating the endomorphism costs substantially less than the doublings it replaces; the paper suggests a practical range below about five point doublings.
For p congruent to 1 modulo 3 and a curve y^2=x^3+b over F_p, an element beta of order three defines the endomorphism (x,y) mapped to (beta*x,y).

For curves passing the listed checks, the paper reports no then-known attack that substantially reduces ECDLP time and characterizes automorphism-related attacks as yielding only a small-factor improvement.

The paper addresses forward scalar multiplication kP on elliptic curves and proposes an efficiency method for that operation; its stated contribution is not an algorithm for recovering k from P and kP.
The GLV approach extends efficient-endomorphism scalar multiplication beyond Koblitz binary curves to a wider class including certain curves over prime fields.

Curves with efficiently computable endomorphisms are special instances and should be reviewed for attacks that exploit their additional structure rather than assumed equivalent to random curves without analysis.
The central decomposition writes k congruent to k1+k2*lambda modulo n with two small components, reducing a full-length scalar multiplication to a simultaneous multiplication using P and phi(P).

The design addresses ECDLP over n-bit prime fields and does not claim the same resource formulas for binary-field curves.

Prime-field point addition reduces to reversible modular arithmetic, with modular inversion through the extended Euclidean algorithm identified as the main technical difficulty.
A power-of-two Fourier transform can replace an exact transform of group order, with constant success probability and classical post-processing approaching unit success.
The implementation is restricted to elliptic curves over GF(p) and does not cover binary or general extension-field representations.
The main presentation assumes a known prime order for the base point, while explaining how quantum order finding and standard reductions handle the more general case.
The extended Euclidean algorithm is reorganized into a reversible computation while controlling variable execution length and retained garbage.
The point-addition circuit uses a simplified generic addition rule and treats exceptional inputs as rare events whose contribution can be bounded.
Finite affine points are represented uniquely by coordinate pairs modulo p, while the implementation is arranged so that a dedicated representation of the point at infinity is not normally required.
The full cryptanalytic circuit is built around controlled affine elliptic-curve point addition and repeated scalar-multiplication steps.
Modular inversion is implemented reversibly through a Montgomery inverse based on the binary extended Euclidean algorithm.
The affine point-addition construction relies on handling or bounding exceptional cases such as zero denominators rather than presenting a universally complete addition law.

Controlled point-addition networks were classically simulated and tested for standard prime curves from 192 through 521 bits.
Constant-point addition is performed in affine coordinates using modular arithmetic and coordinate lookups.
Unlike an undisclosed comparison target discussed in the paper, the circuit design principles are described and implemented in the Qarton framework for reproducibility.
Modular inversion and in-place multiplication are organized by separating a reversible Euclidean-operation transcript from a Bezout-coefficient reconstruction stage.

The reported gate-optimized secp256k1 point-addition circuit uses 1446 logical qubits and fewer Toffoli-class gates than the space-optimized variant.
The reported space-optimized secp256k1 windowed point-addition circuit uses 1192 logical qubits before the small window overhead.
Schrottenloher publishes logical point-addition circuit architectures optimized specifically for secp256k1.
